lessons.title: Setting Up a Full-Coverage Reputation Monitoring System
lessons.title_es: Configurando un sistema de monitoreo de reputación con cobertura total
lessons.description: Build a monitoring stack that catches every mention of your brand across review sites, social media, and the web.
lessons.description_es: Construye un stack de monitoreo que capture cada mención de tu marca en sitios de reseñas, redes sociales y la web.
lessons.content:
Why You Don't Know What People Are Saying

Most businesses monitor one or two platforms and assume they have coverage. In reality, online brand mentions are fragmented across a dozen surfaces: Google reviews, Yelp, industry-specific review sites (Clutch, G2, Capterra), social media (Instagram, Facebook, X/Twitter, TikTok), Reddit threads, news sites, local blogs, and AI chatbot training data that surfaces your brand in response to competitor queries. Missing a negative mention on any of these is a business risk. Missing a positive mention is a missed amplification opportunity.

The Five-Channel Monitoring Stack

Set up monitoring across five categories and route all alerts to a single dashboard or Slack channel:

  • Google Alerts (free): Set up alerts for your exact brand name, common misspellings, your domain name, and your key team members' names. Set delivery to "as it happens" for a business brand. Google Alerts covers news sites, blogs, and some forums.
  • Review platform monitoring: Google Business Profile has built-in review notifications — enable them. For Yelp, Clutch, G2, and industry-specific sites, use a review aggregation tool (Reputation.com, BirdEye, or Podium) that checks all platforms daily and surfaces new reviews in one inbox.
  • Social listening tool: Brand24, Mention, or Sprout Social's listening module monitors Instagram, Facebook, X, Pinterest, TikTok, and YouTube for brand mentions — including mentions in comments and Stories, not just posts. Set keyword triggers for your brand name, your key services, and your main competitors.
  • Reddit and forum monitoring: Reddit search is terrible for brand monitoring. Use a Reddit-specific listener (TrackReddit or Social Search) for your brand name. Also monitor relevant subreddits in your industry — competitor mentions there are market intelligence, not just reputation data.
  • AI chatbot monitoring: Manually query ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews monthly with prompts like "What is [Your Brand]?" and "Is [Your Brand] good?" AI training data lags, but knowing what AI systems say about you is critical as more buyers use AI for vendor research.

Routing Alerts by Severity

Not all mentions require the same response urgency. Triage inbound alerts into three tiers: Immediate (respond within 2 hours) — negative reviews 1–2 stars, public social complaints with significant engagement. Same day (respond within 8 hours) — 3-star reviews, neutral brand mentions that could become positive with engagement. Weekly batch (respond within 5 days) — positive reviews, blog mentions, forum discussions. Build this triage into your monitoring tool's tagging system so the right team member is notified at the right urgency level.

lessons.title: Responding to Reviews: The Playbook
lessons.description: Write review responses that turn critics into advocates and signal quality to future buyers.
lessons.description_es: Escribe respuestas a reseñas que conviertan a los críticos en defensores y señalen calidad a futuros compradores.
lessons.content:
Who Is Your Response Really For?

A counterintuitive truth about review responses: you are not primarily writing for the reviewer. You are writing for the thousands of future buyers who will read the review and your response while evaluating whether to hire you. A thoughtful response to a 1-star review that converts a prospect into a buyer is worth 10× the original negative review's damage.

The Four-Part Response Framework

Every review response — positive or negative — follows this structure:

  1. Acknowledge specifically: Reference something specific from the review to prove you actually read it. "Thank you for mentioning the report format" is better than "Thank you for your feedback." Specificity signals genuine attention.
  2. Validate the experience (negative reviews only): Do not argue, defend, or explain away the experience. Validate it first. "I understand the delay in the first week of the project was frustrating" is not an admission of failure — it's empathy. Defensive responses signal to future buyers that you don't handle criticism well.
  3. State what you've done or will do: For negative reviews, provide a concrete action taken or being taken. "We've since added a weekly status update every Tuesday to prevent the communication gaps you experienced." For positive reviews, reinforce why the experience happened: "Keeping reporting transparent for our clients is something we built into our process from day one."
  4. Invite offline resolution (negative reviews only): Provide a direct contact — not a generic support@ email, but a named person. "Please reach out to [Name] at [email] — I'd like to understand what more we could have done." This moves the conversation off the public platform and demonstrates commitment to resolution.

Using AI to Draft at Scale

For businesses with high review volume, use the Claude API to draft responses. Prompt pattern: "You are responding on behalf of [Business Name], a [type of business]. Tone: warm, professional, non-defensive. Write a response to this [star rating] review: [review text]. Follow this structure: [paste 4-part framework]." Review and personalize the draft before publishing — AI catches the structure; a human adds the authenticity. Never publish a raw AI response without reading it.

lessons.title: Proactive Reputation Building
lessons.description: Generate a consistent stream of positive reviews through systematic client experience design.
lessons.description_es: Genera un flujo constante de reseñas positivas a través del diseño sistemático de la experiencia del cliente.
lessons.content:
Reviews Are Not Luck — They're a Process

Businesses with 4.7+ ratings and hundreds of reviews didn't accumulate them passively. They built a review generation process that runs every time a client reaches a satisfaction milestone. Waiting for happy clients to voluntarily leave reviews results in an over-representation of unhappy clients (who are far more motivated to write). Your job is to activate the silent majority of satisfied clients.

The Review Request Timing Map

The single most important variable in review request conversion is timing. The ask must come at peak satisfaction — not at the end of a long engagement when the client's attention has moved on.

Identify your three highest-satisfaction moments and build review requests into each:

  • Moment 1 — First tangible win: The first deliverable the client is visibly excited about. In an agency context, this might be the first month's campaign results, the finished website design, or the first round of content published. Send the review request within 24 hours of client approval.
  • Moment 2 — Milestone completion: When a project phase completes on time and on budget. Include a brief results summary in the outreach and link to the review platform.
  • Moment 3 — Renewal or upsell: A client who just renewed or expanded their contract has demonstrated strong satisfaction. This is the second-highest-converting review request moment.

The Review Request Message That Works

Short, personal, direct. Never use a generic template that reads like a bulk email:

"Hi [First Name] — seeing [specific result] come together has been genuinely great. If you have 2 minutes, a Google review would mean a lot to us — small businesses like ours live and die by word of mouth. Here's the direct link: [link]. No pressure either way — thanks for being a great client to work with."

The key elements: specific result reference (proves you're not mass-blasting), a 2-minute time estimate (reduces barrier), a direct link (no navigation friction), and a no-pressure close (avoids feeling transactional). Conversion rates for this pattern typically run 25–40% — significantly higher than generic review request automation.

lessons.title: Reputation Recovery: Managing a Crisis
lessons.title_es: Recuperación de reputación: manejando una crisis
lessons.description: Execute a structured response when your reputation takes a significant hit from a viral complaint or negative press.
lessons.description_es: Ejecuta una respuesta estructurada cuando tu reputación sufre un golpe significativo por una queja viral o prensa negativa.
lessons.content:
Defining a Reputation Crisis

Not every bad review is a crisis. A crisis is a reputation event with three characteristics: it has reached or is reaching a significant audience (1,000+ views, trending in a community), it contains specific factual claims (not just dissatisfaction), and it is spreading faster than normal review traffic. Examples: a detailed Reddit thread about your service going negative, a local news story, a viral social complaint from an influencer account, or a coordinated review attack (a competitor or disgruntled ex-employee mobilizing fake reviews).

The 48-Hour Crisis Response Protocol

Time is the primary variable in crisis management — every hour of inactivity allows the narrative to solidify without your voice.

  • Hour 0–2 — Assessment: Identify the source, reach, and content of the crisis. Assign a crisis lead (one person owns the response — committee responses are slow and inconsistent). Determine: Is the underlying complaint factually accurate? Your response strategy differs significantly between a factual complaint and a false one.
  • Hour 2–8 — Initial response: Post a brief, non-defensive acknowledgment in the thread or platform where the crisis originated. Do not provide detailed explanations yet — you don't have all the facts and explanations under pressure read as defensive. The acknowledgment: "We've seen this and we're taking it seriously. [Crisis lead name] is personally reviewing the situation and will follow up directly with [affected party] within 24 hours."
  • Hour 8–24 — Investigation and direct outreach: Contact the affected client or person directly. Understand exactly what happened from their perspective. Prepare a factual account of events from your side. Identify what corrective action is appropriate and possible.
  • Hour 24–48 — Substantive response: Post a full response that: acknowledges the experience, explains (without excusing) what happened, states concrete changes you're making, and provides direct contact information for ongoing resolution. If the complaint is factually inaccurate, correct the record clearly and factually — but only after you've validated what is accurate.

Coordinated Review Attacks: Detection and Response

Review bombing (multiple negative reviews appearing in a short period) is identifiable by pattern: many reviews in 24–48 hours, reviewers with no prior review history, similar language across reviews, and reviews that reference incidents inconsistent with your actual client base. Report these to the platform using their content policy violation tools — Google, Yelp, and G2 all have review removal processes for coordinated inauthentic activity. Document the pattern with screenshots before reporting, as platforms sometimes restore reviews after removal while investigating.
